JenticExecutionId.description: Execution ID — unique identifier for this brokered request.

JenticToolkitId.description: Toolkit ID — the logical grouping the execution belongs to.

Traceparent.description:
W3C Trace Context — `00-{trace-id}-{parent-id}-01`. The
`parent-id` is the broker span; downstream services use this to
link their own spans into the same distributed trace. Version
`00` is current; flags `01` = sampled.

Tracestate.description:
W3C Trace Context vendor extensions. Jentic packs the
execution ID, toolkit ID, and the upstream API reference
(`vendor:name:version`) under the `jentic` vendor key:
`jentic=exec_xyz789:tk_abc123:stripe:payments:2023-10-16`. The
first two segments answer *who is calling*; the trailing
segments answer *what is being called*. Composable with other
vendors (`jentic=...,datadog=...`).

IdempotencyKey.description:
Optional caller-supplied key to make this request idempotent.
If present, the broker dedupes against the key for 24 hours
and replays the original response on retry. UUIDv4 recommended.
See the `Idempotency` section above for full semantics.
IdempotencyKey.name: Idempotency-Key

JenticRevision.description:
Pin upstream-API resolution to a specific `ApiRevision`
instead of the API's `current_revision_id`. Multi-valued —
repeat the header (or comma-separate) once per API you
want to pin. Each value is `vendor:name:version=rev_…`.

Use cases:

- Test a `draft` revision against existing credentials
  before promoting it.
- Reproduce a historical execution against an `archived`
  spec for diagnostic purposes (refused by default;
  archived revisions are not pinnable from the hot path).

The Broker forwards the pin into the control-plane
`POST /lookup/operation` call so URL → `operation_id`
resolution sees the requested spec. When the header is
absent or the URL doesn't match a pinned API, the Broker
resolves against `current_revision_id` as today.

Pinning a `draft` revision the caller doesn't have
access to returns `403`. Pinning an unknown or
`archived` revision returns `422`. The chosen revisions
are recorded on the resulting `ExecutionRecord`
(`pinned_revisions` field on the control plane).
JenticRevision.name: Jentic-Revision

Prefer.description:
RFC 7240 processing preference. `wait={seconds}` — maximum time
to wait for a synchronous response before falling back to async.
`respond-async` — always queue and return `202` immediately.
Prefer.name: Prefer

UpstreamUrl.description: The URL of the upstream service to execute the request to.
UpstreamUrl.name: upstream_url

schema.description: The URL of the upstream service to execute the request to.
schema.title: Upstream Url

BadRequest.description:
Request rejected before reaching the upstream — malformed JSON,
missing required field, or schema-validation failure on the
broker side. Body is RFC 9457 `application/problem+json` using
the shared `ProblemDetails` schema.

Conflict.description:
Idempotency-key conflict (RFC 9457 `application/problem+json`).
One of two cases, distinguished by the problem `type`:

- `idempotency_conflict` — the same `Idempotency-Key` was already
  used with a different request (method, upstream URL, toolkit, or
  body). Reusing a key for a different request is rejected; pick a
  new key.
- `idempotency_in_progress` — a request with this `Idempotency-Key`
  is still being processed. The response carries a `Retry-After`
  header; retry after that delay to obtain the replayed result.

CredentialNotProvisioned.description:
No credential is connected for the resolved upstream API — the
bound credential is a missing dependency, so the broker cannot
sign the upstream call. Body is RFC 9457
`application/problem+json`; the `agent_directive` is a
`prompt_human` instruction carrying a non-secret `provisioning_url`
(when account-linking is configured) and an `intent_id` correlation
id, so an autonomous agent stops and asks the user to connect the
account rather than retrying or guessing the credential.

Default.description: Catch-all problem+json response for any status not enumerated on this operation.

ExecuteAsync.description:
Execution queued. Follow `_links.self` to the control plane
to poll the job and collect the upstream response body once
`status` is `completed`. The body is delivered once and then
evicted from the platform's pickup buffer.

Idempotent-Replayed.description:
Present and `true` when this `202` is a replay of a
previously-queued job under the same `Idempotency-Key`.
The `job_id` will match the original.

Jentic-Api-Vendor.description: API vendor hostname that was called.

Jentic-Operation.description: Name of the operation that was executed.

Preference-Applied.description: RFC 7240 — confirms which processing preference was applied.

schema.description:
Upstream response body, forwarded verbatim. Content type
varies by upstream API — may be JSON, XML, binary, or any
other media type. The `Content-Type` header on the response
reflects what the upstream returned.
schema.summary: Upstream response body forwarded by the Broker.
schema.title: UpstreamBody

ExecuteSync.description:
Synchronous success — upstream responded within the wait window.
The response body and status code are forwarded from the upstream
API.

Upstream response headers are forwarded as-is; broker-added
metadata is confined to the `Jentic-*` namespace.

Idempotent-Replayed.description:
Present and `true` when this response was replayed from a
stored `Idempotency-Key` rather than freshly executed.
Absent on first execution.

Jentic-Api-Vendor.description: API vendor hostname that was called.

Jentic-Operation.description: Name of the operation that was executed.

Jentic-Upstream-Status.description: HTTP status code returned by the upstream API.

Preference-Applied.description: RFC 7240 — confirms which processing preference was applied.

Forbidden.description:
Caller authenticated but not entitled to execute — most often a
token lacking the required `capabilities:execute` scope
(`type: insufficient_scope`), or attempting to pin a `draft`
revision they don't own or call a toolkit they aren't bound to.
Body is RFC 9457 `application/problem+json`.

NotFound.description:
Upstream URL doesn't resolve to a registered API operation, or
the path component refers to a resource the upstream itself
doesn't recognise. Body is RFC 9457
`application/problem+json`.

PayloadTooLarge.description:
Request body exceeded the broker's per-`Content-Type` size cap
(`max_request_bytes_by_type`, falling back to `max_request_bytes`).
The cap is resolved from the request `Content-Type` before the body is
read, so an over-cap upload fails fast. Body is RFC 9457
`application/problem+json`.

ServerError.description:
Unexpected broker-side failure — bug, panic, or unhandled
upstream exception bubble. Should be rare; report with the
`traceparent` header for triage. Body is RFC 9457
`application/problem+json`.

ServiceUnavailable.description:
Broker temporarily unable to serve traffic — startup,
shutdown, dependency outage, or a deliberate maintenance
window. Retry after the interval indicated by `Retry-After`.
Body is RFC 9457 `application/problem+json`.

Retry-After.description:
Seconds to wait before retrying, per RFC 7231 §7.1.3.
May also be sent as an HTTP-date.

TooManyRequests.description:
Caller exceeded their rate-limit window. Retry after the
interval indicated by `Retry-After`; the
`RateLimit-*` headers describe the current quota and
remaining budget per RFC 6648 / `draft-ietf-httpapi-ratelimit-headers`.
Body is RFC 9457 `application/problem+json`.

RateLimit-Limit.description: Total number of requests allowed in the current window.

RateLimit-Remaining.description: Requests remaining in the current window.

Retry-After.description:
Seconds to wait before retrying, per RFC 7231 §7.1.3.
May also be sent as an HTTP-date.

Unauthorized.description:
Authentication missing or invalid — the `Authorization: Bearer
<token>` credential is absent, malformed, expired, revoked, or
(for JWTs) signed by an issuer the Broker doesn't trust. Body is
RFC 9457 `application/problem+json`.

ValidationError.description:
Request well-formed but semantically invalid — e.g. unknown
revision pin, archived revision pin, or a request body that
fails the upstream operation's schema. Field-level details
live in the `errors[]` extension of the
`application/problem+json` body.

AsyncQueuedResponse.description:
Returned as the `202` body on async executions. The Broker
does not expose a polling surface of its own; `_links.self`
is an absolute URL into the platform's control plane, where
the queued work surfaces as a `kind: execution` job. Poll
that URL for status, and follow its `_links.result` to
collect the upstream response body once `status` is
`completed`.

_links.description:
HAL-style link relations for the queued job. `self` always
points at the control-plane job record; the broker does
not expose its own polling surface.

self.description: Absolute URL of the control-plane job record. Poll this for status and result links.

_links.summary: HAL-style links pointing into the control-plane job record.
_links.title: AsyncQueuedResponseLinks

job_id.description: Unique identifier for the queued job. Matches the `job_id` reported by the control plane.
job_id.title: Job Id

AsyncQueuedResponse.summary: Handle returned when the Broker queues an upstream call for async execution.
AsyncQueuedResponse.title: AsyncQueuedResponse

HealthResponse.description:
Body returned by `GET /health` when the Broker is ready to
accept traffic. Shape follows the IETF [Health Check Response
Format for HTTP APIs](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-inadarei-api-health-check)
draft (`application/health+json`). Intentionally minimal —
clients treat `200` + `status: pass` as the green signal; the
body is informational.

status.description:
Health indicator per the IETF Health Check draft.
`pass` — service is healthy. `fail` — service is unhealthy.
`warn` — service is healthy but with concerns. The Broker
returns `pass` on every `200`; `fail` / `warn` are reserved
for future degraded-mode reporting.

version.description: Service build version, mirrors `info.version` in this spec.

HealthResponse.title: HealthResponse

ReadinessResponse.description:
Body returned by `GET /ready`. `status: ready` (HTTP `200`) means the
instance is below the saturation threshold; `status: unready` (HTTP
`503`) means in-flight load is at/near the admission cap and the load
balancer should drain this instance. `saturation` is the current
in-flight count as a fraction of the configured cap.

reason.description: Why the instance is unready (present only on `unready`).

saturation.description: In-flight requests as a fraction of the admission cap.

ReadinessResponse.title: ReadinessResponse

BearerAuth.description:
Credential authorizing a Broker call, sent as
`Authorization: Bearer <token>`. The Broker accepts several
interchangeable credential formats, dispatched by prefix/shape:

- **Opaque access tokens** (`at_…`) — the *same* tokens the control
  plane issues from `POST /oauth/token`. A random string validated by
  server-side lookup (it is **not** a JWT and cannot be decoded by the
  client). Broker and control plane share one token store, so a token
  minted for the control plane authenticates here too.
- **API keys** — agent (`jak_…`) or service-account (`sak_…`) keys.
  May alternatively be sent in the `x-jentic-api-key` header.
- **Toolkit keys** (`jntc_live_…`) — scoped to a single toolkit; they
  carry execute capability implicitly.
- **Self-contained JWTs** — verified by signature (a dev HS256 shared
  secret or a configured trusted issuer with JWKS rotation). The Broker
  is identity-provider-agnostic: the IdP, federation strategy, and
  token-issuance flow are deployment concerns outside this spec.

Every protected operation requires the `capabilities:execute` scope.
Opaque-token, JWT, and API-key callers must carry that scope; toolkit
keys hold it implicitly. Authorization beyond "may execute" (toolkit
binding, revision pinning) is enforced per-request and is not modelled
here.

info.description:
## Overview

The Broker is the platform's stateless data plane. Send it any
HTTP request with the upstream URL as the path; it injects the
caller's stored credentials, forwards method/headers/body as-is,
and returns the upstream response.

```
POST https://broker.your-instance.example/petstore.swagger.io/v2/pet
```

Secrets stay off agents and end-user devices, outbound traffic
has one auditable choke-point, and credentials rotate centrally
without redeploying callers. Sync responses return `200` with the
upstream body; long calls fall back to `202` with a polling link
into the control plane (see `Prefer` on `/{upstream_url}`).

Auth: every operation expects `Authorization: Bearer <token>` except
`GET /health`. Broker-added metadata is confined to the `Jentic-*`
header namespace — anything else on a response belongs to the
upstream.

Toolkit, credential, and API-revision management live on the
[control plane](https://control-plane.example.com), not here.

## Tracing and audit

Every brokered response carries W3C trace context (`traceparent`,
`tracestate`) and a `Jentic-*` header layer with the execution ID
(`exec_…`), toolkit ID (`tk_…`), and upstream API reference
(`vendor:name:version`). OpenTelemetry clients pick this up
automatically; nothing else needs to parse `tracestate`.

To see what happened, ask the control plane:

- `GET /executions/{exec_id}` — full execution metadata (latency,
  status, pinned revisions, caller).
- `GET /executions` — list and filter recent executions.
- `GET /executions/{exec_id}/steps` — per-step trace for workflow
  executions.
- `GET /jobs/{job_id}` and `GET /jobs/{job_id}/result` — async
  job status and pickup of the upstream body.
- `GET /events` / `GET /events/stream` — audit event feed.

Request and response bodies are not persisted by default. Execution
metadata is retained for 30 days (configurable). Body capture is
explicit opt-in. Async response bodies live in a short-lived
pickup buffer collected via `/jobs/{id}/result`, not in the audit
store.
info.summary: Secure execution proxy for upstream APIs — credentials never leave the Broker.
info.title: Broker API

get.description:
Liveness / readiness probe for the Broker service. Returns `200`
with a small `application/health+json` body (IETF Health Check
Response Format draft) when the broker can accept traffic.
Intended for load balancers, container orchestrators, and uptime
monitors — not for human or agent consumption beyond green/red
signalling.

200.description: Service is healthy and ready to accept traffic.

get.description:
Readiness probe distinct from liveness (`/health`). Returns `200`
while the instance can accept traffic and `503` once sustained
in-flight load nears the admission cap, so a load balancer drains
*this* instance before it begins shedding requests itself — without
the pod being restarted (liveness stays green). Intended for the
orchestrator `readinessProbe`, not for human or agent consumption.

200.description: Instance is ready to accept traffic.

503.description: Instance is saturated and should be drained.

delete.description:
Forward a `DELETE` to the upstream service. A request body is
permitted (RFC 7231 does not forbid one) and forwarded verbatim if
present, but most upstreams ignore it. Same response semantics as
`POST`.

requestBody.description: Request body forwarded verbatim to the upstream service.

delete.summary: Execute a `DELETE` against an upstream API

/{upstream_url}.description:
Forward a request to the upstream API URL specified in the path. The
HTTP method you use on this endpoint is the method the broker sends to
the upstream service — every standard method (`GET`, `POST`, `PUT`,
`PATCH`, `DELETE`, `HEAD`, `OPTIONS`) is supported.

Request headers and body are forwarded as-is. The broker injects the
caller's stored credentials for the target API.

The path, query, and header parameters listed below apply to every
method. Per-method operations only differ in semantic intent and (for
methods that conventionally carry a body) in `requestBody` shape.

### Sync vs async

By default, the broker waits up to 30 seconds for the upstream
response. Control this with the `Prefer` header:

- `Prefer: wait=30` — wait up to 30 seconds, then fall back to async.
- `Prefer: respond-async` — always queue and return `202` immediately.

Async executions return a `202` with a polling link into the control plane.

### Error handling

Platform-level errors (auth failures, missing credentials) return an
RFC 9457 Problem Details (`application/problem+json`) body using the
shared `ProblemDetails` schema from
[jentic/api-problem-details](https://github.com/jentic/api-problem-details).
Field-level validation failures populate the `errors[]` extension.

### Idempotency

Optional `Idempotency-Key` request header (IETF
draft-ietf-httpapi-idempotency-key). If present, the broker
records the key for 24 hours and replays the original response
for any subsequent request that reuses it — including `202`
handles, so retrying a queued async request returns the *same*
`job_id` instead of double-queueing the upstream call. Keys are
scoped per-caller and should be unique per logical operation
(UUIDv4 recommended, max 255 chars).

Replays are signalled with `Idempotent-Replayed: true` on the
response. If the same key is reused with a different request
body or upstream URL, the broker returns `409 Conflict`.

### Proxied upstream headers

On synchronous `200` responses, upstream API response headers are
forwarded as-is. Hop-by-hop headers (RFC 7230 §6.1) and the
broker's own trace/metadata headers are not proxied — every
broker-added header lives under the `Jentic-*` namespace, so
`Jentic-*` is unambiguously the broker's and anything else
belongs to the upstream.

### Security

The Broker validates every `upstream_url` against the set of
registered APIs before forwarding. Arbitrary URLs that do not
resolve to a known, registered API endpoint are rejected with
`404` — the Broker is **not** an open proxy. This allowlist
check is the spec's primary SSRF mitigation boundary:
credentials are never injected into requests targeting
unregistered hosts.

### Revision pinning

By default the Broker resolves the upstream URL against the
target API's `current_revision_id` (the `published` revision
registered on the control plane). To test a `draft` revision
with real credentials before promoting it, attach the
`Jentic-Revision` request header. The header is
multi-valued — repeat it (or comma-separate) once per API
you want to pin, with each value of the form
`vendor:name:version=rev_…`.

The Broker forwards the pin into the control-plane
`POST /lookup/operation` call so URL → `operation_id`
resolution sees the requested spec. When the header is
absent or the URL doesn't match a pinned API, the Broker
resolves against `current_revision_id` as today.

Pinning a `draft` revision the caller doesn't have access to
returns `403`. Pinning an unknown or `archived` revision
returns `422` — `archived` revisions are deliberately not
pinnable from the hot path; resurrect them by re-promoting
instead. The chosen revisions are recorded on the resulting
`ExecutionRecord` (`pinned_revisions` field on the control
plane) so audit can answer "did this execution use a pinned
revision?" without parsing headers.

get.description:
Forward a `GET` to the upstream service. No request body. Typically
used for resource reads and listings; the upstream response is
returned verbatim on `200`, or queued and surfaced via `202` when
the wait window is exceeded or `Prefer: respond-async` is set.
get.summary: Execute a `GET` against an upstream API

head.description:
Forward a `HEAD` to the upstream service. No request body, no
response body — only headers. Useful for cheap existence /
last-modified checks. Async (`202`) is unusual for `HEAD` but
permitted for parity with the other methods.
head.summary: Execute a `HEAD` against an upstream API

options.description:
Forward an `OPTIONS` to the upstream service. Typically used for
CORS preflight discovery and capability probing. Same response
semantics as `GET`.
options.summary: Execute an `OPTIONS` against an upstream API

patch.description:
Forward a `PATCH` to the upstream service. Request body is forwarded
verbatim. Same response semantics as `POST`.

requestBody.description: Request body forwarded verbatim to the upstream service.

patch.summary: Execute a `PATCH` against an upstream API

post.description:
Forward a `POST` to the upstream service. Request body is forwarded
verbatim — the Broker does not inspect or transform it. The upstream
response is returned on `200`, or queued and surfaced via `202` when
the wait window is exceeded or `Prefer: respond-async` is set.

requestBody.description: Request body forwarded verbatim to the upstream service.

post.summary: Execute a `POST` against an upstream API

put.description:
Forward a `PUT` to the upstream service. Request body is forwarded
verbatim. Same response semantics as `POST`.

requestBody.description: Request body forwarded verbatim to the upstream service.

put.summary: Execute a `PUT` against an upstream API

servers.description: Production (placeholder — set to your deployment's Broker host)
Development (placeholder — set to your deployment's Broker host)

tags.description:
Proxy upstream API operations through the Broker, and inspect the
metadata records they leave behind for audit, latency analysis,
and failure diagnosis.
tags.name: Executions

tags.description:
Operational endpoints for liveness, readiness, and platform
observability — not part of the brokered execution surface.
tags.name: System
